class_schedule.topic: Cost Management Framework and Estimation Foundations
learning_objectives: Describe the cost management knowledge area and its processes
Differentiate between direct costs, indirect costs, and overhead
Understand the relationship between cost, scope, and schedule baselines

lecture_notes.introduction: Cost management ensures the project is completed within the approved budget. It spans estimating, budgeting, and controlling costs.
key_concepts: Cost management plan: how costs will be estimated, budgeted, managed, and controlled
Direct costs: labor, materials, equipment directly attributable to the project
Indirect costs: overhead, facilities, administrative support shared across projects
Fixed vs. variable costs
Sunk costs: already spent, should not influence future decisions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Categorizing costs from a real project budget into direct, indirect, fixed, and variable

review.value: Direct costs are traceable to a specific project; indirect costs are shared and allocated proportionally
Money already spent should not influence decisions about continuing or canceling a project

learning_objectives: Apply analogous, parametric, and bottom-up estimating to cost
Calculate cost estimates with contingency reserves
Understand the accuracy range at each project phase
readings: Humphreys, Project and Cost Engineers Handbook, Ch. 9

lecture_notes.introduction: Cost estimation builds on the WBS. Each work package needs a cost estimate, and these roll up to the project budget.
key_concepts: Analogous estimating: quick but least accurate, based on similar past projects
Parametric estimating: uses cost per unit metrics (e.g., $150/sq ft for construction)
Bottom-up estimating: detailed estimates for each work package, most accurate
Contingency reserve: known-unknowns, included in the cost baseline
Management reserve: unknown-unknowns, not in the cost baseline but in the budget
lecture_notes.demonstration: Bottom-up cost estimate for a 20-activity software project using resource rates and durations

review.value: Most accurate method; estimates each work package individually and aggregates upward
Contingency reserves cover known risks (in baseline); management reserves cover unknowns (outside baseline)

learning_objectives: Aggregate cost estimates into a project budget
Differentiate between cost baseline, project budget, and funding requirements

lecture_notes.introduction: The cost baseline is the time-phased budget that serves as the reference for measuring cost performance throughout the project.
key_concepts: Cost aggregation: rolling up work package estimates to control accounts and project level
Cost baseline: time-phased spending plan, displayed as an S-curve
Project budget = cost baseline + management reserve
Funding requirements: stepped funding aligned with organizational budget cycles
Reconciling top-down budget constraints with bottom-up estimates
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a cost baseline S-curve in a spreadsheet using monthly expenditure data
setup_requirements: Completed bottom-up estimate from previous session

review.value: Cumulative cost plot over time; slow start, steep middle, flattening at end; used to compare planned vs. actual spending
Cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget; adding management reserve gives the total project budget

learning_objectives: Calculate PV, EV, AC, CV, SV, CPI, and SPI
Interpret EVM metrics to assess project health
Forecast final project cost using EAC formulas
readings: Fleming & Koppelman, Earned Value Project Management, Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Earned Value Management is the gold standard for integrated cost and schedule performance measurement.
key_concepts: Planned Value (PV): budgeted cost of work scheduled
Earned Value (EV): budgeted cost of work performed
Actual Cost (AC): actual cost of work performed
Cost Variance (CV) = EV - AC; Schedule Variance (SV) = EV - PV
CPI = EV/AC; SPI = EV/PV; EAC = BAC/CPI (most common formula)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Complete EVM analysis of a project at month 6 with provided PV, EV, and AC data

review.value: CPI > 1.0 means under budget (getting more value per dollar); CPI < 1.0 means over budget
EAC = BAC/CPI assumes future performance matches current CPI; EAC = AC + (BAC-EV) assumes future matches plan

learning_objectives: Apply multiple EAC formulas for different scenarios
Calculate TCPI for budget and deadline targets
Analyze EVM trends using cumulative CPI and SPI charts
readings: PMI Practice Standard for Earned Value Management, Ch. 4

lecture_notes.introduction: Different EAC formulas are appropriate for different situations. Knowing which to apply requires judgment about whether past performance will continue.
key_concepts: EAC = AC + (BAC - EV): future work at planned rate
EAC = BAC / CPI: future work at current efficiency
EAC = AC + (BAC - EV) / (CPI x SPI): accounts for both cost and schedule performance
TCPI = (BAC - EV) / (BAC - AC): efficiency needed to meet budget
TCPI against EAC: efficiency needed to meet the new estimate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing three EAC calculations for a project and recommending which is most appropriate
setup_requirements: EVM dataset with 12 months of PV, EV, AC data

review.value: To-Complete Performance Index shows the required future efficiency; TCPI > 1.0 means the team must improve
Plotting cumulative CPI and SPI over time reveals whether performance is improving, stable, or declining

learning_objectives: Implement cost control processes within integrated change control
Analyze cost variances and determine root causes
Apply corrective and preventive actions for cost overruns

lecture_notes.introduction: Cost control is not about cutting costs after problems arise; it is about proactively managing spending against the baseline.
key_concepts: Cost change control: requests must go through integrated change control
Variance analysis: comparing planned vs. actual at control account level
Root cause analysis: why did the variance occur?
Corrective actions: bring future performance back in line
Preventive actions: avoid anticipated cost problems before they occur
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study: diagnosing cost overruns in a construction project and recommending corrective actions

review.value: Organizations set thresholds (e.g., +/- 10%) that trigger formal investigation and corrective action
All changes to the cost baseline must go through the change control board for approval

questions.question: A project has the following work packages: WP1 costs $15,000 (labor) + $5,000 (materials), WP2 costs $20,000 (labor) + $8,000 (materials), WP3 costs $12,000 (labor) + $3,000 (materials). The contingency reserve is 10% of direct costs. Calculate the cost baseline.
Explain why sunk costs should not influence project continuation decisions and provide a real-world example.
questions.answer: Sunk costs are costs already incurred that cannot be recovered regardless of future decisions. They should not influence decisions because rational decision-making compares future costs and benefits only. Example: A company has spent $2M developing a product that market research now shows will only generate $500K in revenue. The $2M is sunk; the correct decision is to cancel the project rather than spending more to complete it, because the future investment would also yield a loss.

rubric.key: Explains why they should be ignored in decisions

questions.question: A project has BAC = $200,000. At month 8, PV = $120,000, EV = $100,000, AC = $130,000. Calculate CV, SV, CPI, SPI, and EAC (using CPI method).
Using the data from the previous question, calculate TCPI (against BAC) and interpret what it means for the project team.
questions.answer: TCPI = (BAC - EV) / (BAC - AC) = ($200,000 - $100,000) / ($200,000 - $130,000) = $100,000 / $70,000 = 1.43. This means the team must perform the remaining work at 143% efficiency (getting $1.43 of value for every $1.00 spent) to finish on budget. Since this is significantly above 1.0 and the current CPI is only 0.77, meeting the original budget is highly unlikely.

major_assignments.title: Project Cost Estimate and EVM Analysis
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete bottom-up cost estimate for a project of your choice, construct the cost baseline, then perform a simulated EVM analysis at three reporting periods.
objectives: Create a bottom-up cost estimate from a WBS
Build a time-phased cost baseline with S-curve
Perform EVM calculations for three reporting periods
Forecast final cost using appropriate EAC formulas
deliverables: Bottom-up cost estimate spreadsheet with resource rates and durations
EVM analysis report with PV, EV, AC, CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, and TCPI for three periods
Management briefing memo (2 pages) summarizing project cost health and recommendations

criteria: All work packages have detailed cost estimates
Resource rates and durations are realistic
Contingency and management reserves are properly calculated
All EVM metrics correctly calculated for three periods
Appropriate EAC formula selected and justified
Trend analysis shows performance over time
Clear summary of project cost health
Complete and realistic bottom-up cost estimate
Professional management briefing with actionable recommendations

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on cost estimating, budgeting, and EVM calculations

cost_project.description: Comprehensive cost estimate and EVM analysis assignment

midterm_examination.description: Cost estimation techniques and budgeting concepts

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam with emphasis on EVM calculations and forecasting

class_participation.description: Participation in in-class exercises and case study discussions

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences will result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: The cost project is individual work. Study groups are encouraged for exam preparation but submitted work must be original.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Properly cite all sources. Using another student's calculations without attribution is considered plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to a spreadsheet application (Excel or Google Sheets) and a calculator. Familiarity with Microsoft Project from PM102 is assumed.
